skill.name: auth-implementation-patterns
skill.description: Guides authentication and authorization implementation for JWT, OAuth2, session cookies, RBAC, permissions, ownership checks, password security, and rate limiting.
skill.summary: Plan and review secure authentication and authorization patterns for web applications and APIs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings were mostly false positives from fenced TypeScript examples, Markdown backticks, normal environment variable reads, and defensive guidance against localStorage. One real issue was found: the OAuth callback example redirects with an access token in the URL query string, which can leak through browser history, logs, and referrers. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential theft, or executable skill code was found.

medium_findings.title: Access Token Exposed In OAuth Redirect URL
medium_findings.description: The OAuth callback example redirects to the frontend with an access token in the query string. Query tokens can be stored in browser history, server logs, analytics, and referrer headers. Use an authorization code flow, secure server-side exchange, or httpOnly secure cookies instead.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line explicitly appends tokens.accessToken to a redirect URL. This is a known token leakage pattern, although it appears in educational example code rather than executable skill logic.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Fenced Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown code fence markers or inline resource references, not Ruby or shell execution. They do not execute commands during skill use, but they remain tracked as static risk-factor evidence because examples contain runnable application snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown fences or documentation references. No executable script, hook, or command invocation was found in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Reads Are Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The environment variable detections show example application code reading JWT secrets, session secrets, Redis URLs, OAuth client secrets, and frontend URLs. This is normal for authentication implementation guidance and no exfiltration path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside TypeScript examples and use environment variables as local configuration. I found no evidence that the skill reads or transmits the evaluator environment.

low_findings.title: Browser Credential And Storage Findings Are Defensive Guidance
low_findings.description: The browser credential and storage detections are best-practice text recommending secure cookies and warning that JWT storage in localStorage is vulnerable to XSS. These are not attempts to access browser credentials or storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain guidance, including an explicit warning against localStorage token storage. No browser file path or storage access code was present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections correspond to JWT, RBAC, or permission examples rather than MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak algorithms. The password guidance uses bcrypt with a cost factor and recommends argon2 as an option.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines do not contain weak cryptographic algorithm calls. The security section shows bcrypt usage and does not recommend obsolete hashing or encryption.

dangerous_patterns.title: Token In Query String
dangerous_patterns.description: A sample OAuth callback places an access token in a redirect query parameter. This pattern should be replaced before publication or clearly warned against in the marketplace listing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token is interpolated directly into the callback URL. The surrounding code confirms it is an OAuth login flow example.

content.value_statement: Authentication code is easy to get wrong and hard to review after release. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan safer login, token, session, and authorization flows.
actual_capabilities: Explains JWT access and refresh token patterns for Express applications.
Shows session cookie setup with Redis-backed session storage.
Outlines OAuth2 social login with Passport.js examples.
Provides RBAC, permission, and resource ownership enforcement patterns.
Covers password validation, password hashing, and rate limiting examples.
Lists practical auth best practices and common implementation pitfalls.
limitations: Examples are TypeScript-oriented and assume Express-like application structure.
The skill provides guidance, not a complete production authentication service.
It does not validate compliance with specific regulatory frameworks.
One OAuth example should be adjusted to avoid access tokens in redirect URLs.

use_cases.title: Design API Authentication
use_cases.description: Choose JWT, session, or OAuth2 patterns for a new API and document token lifecycle decisions.

use_cases.title: Review Access Control
use_cases.description: Check RBAC, permission checks, and ownership rules before releasing protected endpoints.

use_cases.title: Harden Login Flows
use_cases.description: Add password policy, rate limiting, secure cookie settings, and safer logout behavior to existing login flows.

prompt_templates.title: Choose An Auth Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new application
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to compare JWT, session cookies, and OAuth2 for my app. Ask about users, clients, deployment, and security needs before recommending a path.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Protected Routes
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design protected API routes for these roles and resources. Include authentication middleware, authorization checks, and failure responses.

prompt_templates.title: Review Token Lifecycle
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my access token, refresh token, logout, and rotation design. Identify leakage risks, replay risks, and storage weaknesses.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Full Auth Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit this authentication architecture end to end. Cover threat model, session storage, OAuth callbacks, RBAC, ownership checks, logging, and rollout risks.

output_examples.input: I need secure auth for a SaaS API with admins, members, and invited users.
output: A recommended session or token strategy with lifecycle notes.
A role and permission model for each protected action.
A checklist for secrets, cookies, logging, rate limits, and ownership checks.

output_examples.input: Review my OAuth login flow before launch.
output: A callback flow review focused on token handling and redirect safety.
Findings about state validation, secure storage, account linking, and logout.
Concrete remediation steps prioritized by release risk.

output_examples.input: Our app has JWT refresh tokens and users report stale permissions.
output: A diagnosis plan for token claims, refresh behavior, revocation, and permission caching.
Safer alternatives for short-lived access tokens and server-side authorization checks.

best_practices: Start with a threat model that names clients, tenants, secrets, and protected resources.
Prefer secure httpOnly cookies or server-side token exchange for browser-based OAuth flows.
Review authentication, authorization, storage, logging, and rate limits together before release.
anti_patterns: Do not place access tokens in redirect URLs or long-lived browser storage.
Do not rely on client-side role checks without server-side enforcement.
Do not copy examples into production without adapting secrets, cookies, CSRF, and revocation behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill write production-ready authentication code?
faq.answer: It provides implementation patterns and review guidance. Teams must adapt and test the code for their stack and threat model.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What frameworks does it focus on?
faq.answer: The examples focus on TypeScript, Express, Passport.js, Redis sessions, bcrypt, and common API patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover authorization as well as login?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes RBAC, permissions, and resource ownership checks for protected endpoints.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: Most static findings are false positives, but one OAuth example places an access token in a URL query string.

faq.question: What should users verify before release?
faq.answer: Verify token storage, redirect handling, cookie flags, CSRF protections, secret rotation, rate limits, and audit logging.
